comments.text: [El19] is unable to load the reference.

The constant in the upper bound has also been improved slightly via R(3;k). Wan (1997) proved $R(3;k)\leq \tfrac{e−e^{-1}+3}{2}\cdot k!+1$, and Xu Xiaodong, Xie Zheng and Chen Zhi (2002) improved this to $R(3;k) \leq (e−\tfrac{1}{6})\cdot k!+1$. The latter paper is in Chinese and I haven't been able to open the file, but Eliahou (2019) contains a write-up in English, along with some bounds conditional on improvements for small k. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Same comment as for problem 183: The mentioned base of the lower bound $(321)^{1/5}\approx 3.1717$ of Exoo (1994) was improved to $(1073)^{1/6} \approx3.1996$ by Fredericksen and Sweet (2000) doi:10.37236/1510 via $f(6)\geq 536$. Both papers used that $\lim_{k \rightarrow \infty}f(k)^{1/k} \geq (2 f(m)+1)^{1/m}$ for every positive integer $m$; see e.g. Corollary 2.1 in Abbott and Hanson (1972). The current best lower bound is in a preprint of Ageron, Casteras, Pellerin, Portella, Rimmel and Tomasik (2021) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2112.03175, where they find $\lim_{k \rightarrow \infty} f(k)^{1/k} \geq (380)^{1/5} \approx 3.2806\ldots$ (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

pedantically, I do not think Schur noted that $f(k)\le R(3;k)-1$. he basically recreated the now standard proof of $R(3;k)\le e k!$ without defining a graph, instead directly working with additive sets. so arguably it is an "implicit observation" or something. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
